calendar.event: Treasury coupon interest payment window; large reserve add

narrative.regime_text: Receipts are outpacing spending or the impulse trend is weakening. This drains reserves and tends to coincide with tighter front-end funding.

lane_decomposition.caveat: Composition observation only. Pre-2023-02-14 the Treasury commingled withheld FICA and corporate income tax in a single FTD bucket; lane split is clean from FY2024 onward. Lane signal has no forward equity edge: see dead-ends/dts_project.md.

rules.entry: T+10 trading days after first cluster day

macro_setups.caveat: Regime indicators (exposure tilts), not discrete trade triggers. Historical bucket stats are unconditional 120d forward SPX averages across 5,000+ trading days; entering at the start of a fresh regime may not capture the full edge. Use alongside Tier-1 timing system.

cr_status.message: No full-year appropriations found. Government operating under CR.

report.framework: This report applies an operational lens consistent with the accounting of the Federal Reserve and the US Treasury as described in the Daily Treasury Statement. Government spending = the Treasury instructs the Fed to credit reserve accounts (dollars created). Taxes and tariffs = the Fed debits reserve accounts (dollars destroyed). Securities issuance = the Fed debits reserve accounts and credits securities accounts (reserve drain, not borrowing). Securities redemption = the Fed debits securities accounts and credits reserve accounts (reserve add). The deficit = net dollars added to the non-government sector = the non-government sector's net savings in dollar financial assets. The TGA = a timing buffer between spending and collection, not a constraint on the government's ability to spend.
report.executive_summary: As of 2026-04-24, the fiscal-flow regime reads **Restrictive** for the non-government sector. Net fiscal transfer momentum is **accelerating**: 5d average is $-7.8bn versus prior-20d average $-12.8bn. MTD net transfers are $-254.4bn, with YoY comparable at +18.1%. FYTD, the government has added $724.6bn in net financial assets to the non-government sector (-13.4% YoY); this is the net dollars spent that have not yet been used to pay taxes or purchase securities. The TGA closed at $974.3bn. Treasury spending has exceeded collections recently, drawing down the TGA and crediting reserves to the banking system.
report.what_changed: Over the last 5 sessions, average net fiscal transfer was $-7.8bn per day, versus $-12.8bn in the prior 20-session baseline. Tax drains averaged $30.6bn versus $41.7bn, while net withdrawals averaged $29.3bn against $33.5bn. The largest daily move in the 20-day window was 2026-04-15 at $-98.7bn. On balance, weekly flow momentum is improving relative to the recent baseline.
scorecard_items: MTD net fiscal transfer (spending minus taxes/tariffs): $-254.4bn (+18.1% YoY comparable).
FYTD net financial assets added to non-government sector: $724.6bn (-13.4% YoY comparable).
MTD government spending (reserve credits): $613.8bn vs MTD tax/tariff drains (reserve debits): $868.1bn.
MTD household transfers (dollars created â†' households): $163.9bn (+4.7% YoY).
MTD interest income to bondholders (dollars created â†' savers): $11.0bn (-70.3% YoY).
MTD tax refunds (dollars returned â†' households): $89.1bn (+18.5% YoY).
report.drivers: Relative to the comparable month last year, the shift is primarily receipt-led. Government spending (reserve credits) moved by $-59.2bn while tax and tariff drains (reserve debits) moved by $-115.5bn. The FYTD operating deficit; dollars spent by the government that have not yet been used to pay taxes. is $724.6bn, versus $836.6bn at this point last year. This is identically the net financial assets added to the non-government sector's savings. Tariff collections are running +190.1% above prior year at $204.8bn FYTD, now representing 5.3% of total deposit drains. Tariffs are operationally identical to taxes: they destroy dollars. When the government collects a tariff, the Fed debits the importer's bank's reserve account; those dollars cease to exist in the private sector. Monthly tariff drains are approximately 0.18x the monthly SSA payment cycle. While tariffs are not yet cancelling out SSA payments in aggregate, the daily tariff collection trend is creeping higher and worth monitoring. both are direct dollar flows between the government and households, in opposite directions. Interest payments to bondholders are running at approximately $664.6bn on a trailing 12-month basis, representing 7.7% of total government spending. This is one of the largest single fiscal injection channels: when Treasury pays interest, the Fed credits reserve accounts of bondholder banks; these are new dollars entering the private sector. As the white paper describes, a positive policy rate is effectively "basic income for those who already have money." The higher the debt/GDP, the larger the fiscal impact of rate policy. This creates a paradox: what the Fed calls "tightening" simultaneously injects more dollars into the economy through the interest income channel, partially offsetting the intended demand reduction. Total FYTD government spending is +1.9% versus prior year, broadly on a similar trajectory to FY2025 despite headlines about spending cuts. The aggregate flow data does not yet show a material reduction in the rate at which the government is crediting reserve accounts.

signals.title: Refund Season Tracker
signals.text: FYTD tax refunds issued: $332.7bn (+18.1% YoY). Refunds are government spending; the Treasury instructs the Fed to credit recipient bank reserve accounts. These are new dollars entering the private sector, making refund season the largest predictable household cash injection in Q1. FYTD pace is tracking close to prior year; broadly normal rate of household dollar injection. The trailing 4-week pace is $0.1bn vs $0.1bn same window prior year (+41.0% YoY). The recent pace has turned positive year-over-year even while the FYTD cumulative remains below prior year; this is the inflection point that matters for near-term household cash flow. The cumulative hole was dug earlier in the fiscal year; the current run-rate is now injecting dollars at a faster rate than at the same point last year.

signals.title: Government Account Series Divergence
signals.text: Net GAS issuance is $342.5bn vs $-69.8bn at this point last year (+590.7%). Large shifts in GAS often indicate extraordinary measures during debt ceiling episodes, or significant trust fund rebalancing. This divergence warrants attention in the context of any upcoming debt ceiling deadlines.

signals.title: Tariff Revenue Acceleration
signals.text: Tariff/customs receipts FYTD: $204.8bn (+190.1% YoY). Tariffs now represent 5.3% of total deposit drains. Operationally, tariffs are identical to taxes: when collected, the Fed debits the importer's bank's reserve account; those dollars are destroyed. The economy needs the government to spend or lend those dollars back into existence before they can circulate again. The DTS does not distinguish policy tariff changes from routine customs flows, but the magnitude of the YoY shift is consistent with the new tariff regime taking effect, directly reducing private-sector dollar balances.

signals.title: Interest Income Channel & Rate Paradox
signals.text: Trailing 12-month interest payments to bondholders: approximately $664.6bn, representing 7.7% of all government spending. When Treasury pays coupon interest, the Fed credits reserve accounts; this is new money entering the economy, functionally identical to any other government spending. At current policy rates, this channel operates as what Warren Mosler describes as "basic income for those who already have money." The paradox: what the Fed calls "tightening" simultaneously increases this fiscal injection. The higher the debt/GDP ratio, the larger the fiscal impact of rate policy. If rates remain elevated and the debt continues to roll at higher coupons, interest expense will continue climbing toward and potentially beyond $1 trillion annually. a massive, regressive fiscal stimulus running on autopilot through the interest income channel.

report.base_case: The current restrictive regime persists, with net transfers running below prior-year comparable pace. This setup is more likely to leave growth and liquidity mildly constrained than meaningfully boosted.
report.alt_scenario: Conditions improve if withdrawals outpace receipts and 5d net-transfer averages remain above 20d averages. Confirmation would be improving MTD and FYTD YoY deltas plus repeated positive net-transfer surprises.
report.falsification: Sustained negative YoY net-transfer prints alongside elevated receipt leakage.
forward_calendar: **2026-05-15:** Treasury coupon interest payment window; large reserve add
report.forward_text: The April 15 individual income tax deadline is the single largest dollar-destruction event of the year. When taxpayers pay, the Fed debits their banks' reserve accounts; typically $150-300bn in a single day. These dollars cease to exist in the private sector. Front-end rates will firm into this date as the private sector scrambles to obtain the dollars needed to meet the obligation, and ease after. The next major Treasury coupon interest payment window is around May 15. When Treasury pays coupon interest, the Fed credits bondholder banks' reserve accounts. a large one-day dollar injection into the private sector. At the current interest expense run-rate (~$664.6bn/yr), these periodic payments are among the largest single-day fiscal injections on the calendar.

market_map.reason: Net fiscal transfers below prior year, but partially offset by the interest income channel (~$664.6bn/yr to bondholders). The fiscal drag from slower spending is real, but the rate paradox means "tightening" simultaneously injects dollars through interest.
Net bill paydowns ($-193.8bn). When bills mature without full rollover, the Fed credits reserve accounts; returning dollars to the banking system, compressing front-end spreads.

Heavy 20d coupon issuance ($78.2bn). Notes and bonds drain reserves from the banking system and add duration to private portfolios. This is not the government "borrowing"; it is a reserve drain that occurs after spending has already credited reserves. But the term premium effect is real: more duration supply pushes yields higher.
Mixed fiscal signals; no strong directional USD read from flows
Mixed reserve signals; fiscal and TGA effects partially offset

margin.contrarian: Speculative leverage is elevated — approaching but not yet at extremes. Watch for trend reversal as the leading signal.
margin.narrative: FINRA margin debt as of 2026-03 is $1220.9bn (+38.7% YoY, trend: rising). Leverage is **elevated leverage** (z-score: 1.40) — approaching but not yet at extreme territory. Elevated speculative leverage into a restrictive fiscal regime. margin-funded positions are vulnerable if fiscal injection continues declining.

fred_refs.reason: TGA at $974.3bn. spending exceeding collections (reserve add). TGA is the timing buffer between government spending and collection.

fred_refs.reason: Tariffs running +190.1% YoY; these destroy private-sector dollars identically to taxes. Verify scale against quarterly NIPA data.

fred_refs.reason: Net fiscal transfers declining + TGA rebuild = fewer dollars in reserve accounts. Check if aggregate reserves are compressing.

fred_refs.reason: Total outstanding public debt = total dollars spent by government not yet used to pay taxes. Context for GAS/extraordinary measures and the interest income channel scale.

caveats: Seasonal tax dates, refunds, and benefit cycles create temporary volatility in daily flow reads.
Current month is partial until month end, so MTD inferences are provisional.
FYTD reads are matched by fiscal month index for apples-to-apples YoY comparisons.
Tariff/customs categories include broader customs receipts, not only policy tariff changes; but operationally they all destroy dollars identically.
Interest payments add dollars to the private sector in aggregate, but transmission to spending demand is distribution-dependent; bondholders have a higher savings propensity than transfer recipients.

The public debt is not a liability in the household sense. It is the accounting record of net dollars the government has spent that have not yet been used to pay taxes; it equals the non-government sector's net savings of dollar financial assets.
Securities issuance is a reserve drain, not borrowing. The government spends first (credits reserves), then offers securities which debit reserves and credit securities accounts at the Fed.
report.policy_monitor:
• DHS funding standoff resolution (June 1 deadline); ICE/Border Patrol appropriation acceleration. DTS line: discretionary outlays for homeland security (DHS payroll, operations, enforcement) and potentially higher customs receipts if immigration enforcement spending catalyzes border activity reporting. Timing: near-term (next 5 weeks); structural if combined with tariff/enforcement regime shift. Current restrictive fiscal regime may reverse sharply on passage, shifting to expansionary injection as Congress funds backdated obligations.

• Higher front-end bill rates (13-week and 26-week bills both allotted at 46.78% and 75.92% of high yield) signal tight bidding; TGA rebuild underway. DTS line: Treasury General Account balance trajectory and reserve drain mechanics. Timing: imminent (settlement April 30, 2026); if bills roll and TGA holding pattern continues, front-end funding pressure persists and may force accelerated TGA drawdown or near-term note issuance concentration. Monitor whether the 5-year weakness (61% at high) indicates demand drought lengthening maturity curve.

• White House capital projects (White House ballroom construction, Lafayette Park beautification) framed as "on budget, ahead of schedule." DTS line: federal construction outlays and potential supplemental spending authority if projects require emergency appropriations or interagency transfers. Timing: structural (multi-month execution); low immediate DTS impact unless these trigger supplemental budget authority requests that clash with the June 1 DHS deadline, fragmenting legislative bandwidth and delaying revenue-raising measures (tariffs, enforcement collections).

• Oil price spike (Brent >$108/bbl after Iran peace talks unravel) absent corresponding tariff/energy tax revenue signal in DTS. DTS line: federal energy tax receipts (if excise taxes on fuel apply) remain latent until market shock translates to tax statute changes or supply-side fiscal response. Timing: monitoring lag; no direct DTS impact until Administration tightens or expands energy taxation or defense spending follows geopolitical escalation. Current restrictive regime does not yet reflect energy shock pass-through.
